title: Home & Family
shortSummary: Describe your home, rooms, and furniture. Talk about your family members, relationships, and living situation.
culturalNote: Family is the cornerstone of Hispanic culture. It's common for adult children to live with their parents until marriage, and multigenerational households are normal. Extended family gatherings every weekend are a tradition. The concept of 'familia' often extends beyond blood relatives to include close family friends, who might be called 'tío' or 'tía' as a sign of affection.

keyPhrases.context: 'Piso' in Spain, 'apartamento' or 'departamento' in Latin America
'Hermanos' can mean brothers or siblings (mixed gender)
Using possessives to talk about family

keyPhrases.spanish: Mi habitación tiene una cama doble
keyPhrases.english: My room has a double bed
keyPhrases.pronunciation: mee ah-bee-tah-see-OHN tee-EH-neh OO-nah KAH-mah DOH-bleh

keyPhrases.spanish: Mi hermana mayor vive en Madrid
keyPhrases.english: My older sister lives in Madrid
keyPhrases.pronunciation: mee ehr-MAH-nah mah-YOHR BEE-beh ehn mah-DREED
keyPhrases.context: Mayor = older, menor = younger for siblings
Common among young people in Spanish cities

keyPhrases.spanish: El salón tiene un sofá y una tele
keyPhrases.english: The living room has a sofa and a TV
keyPhrases.pronunciation: ehl sah-LOHN tee-EH-neh oon soh-FAH ee OO-nah TEH-leh

keyPhrases.context: Extended family is often geographically close

grammar.rule: Possessive adjectives: mi, tu, su, nuestro
grammar.explanation: Possessive adjectives go before the noun and agree in number (not gender, except nuestro/vuestro): mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus, nuestro(a)/nuestros(as), vuestro(a)/vuestros(as), su/sus.
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting plural: 'mi padres' should be 'mis padres' — the possessive must match the noun's number.

grammar.rule: Tener for describing what you have/own
grammar.explanation: 'Tener' (to have) is used to describe possessions, family members, and room contents. It's irregular: tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen.
grammar.commonMistake: Confusing 'tiene' (he/she/it has) with 'tienes' (you have) — the 's' matters.

dialogue.setup: Rosa shows her new flatmate Pablo around their shared apartment.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.spanish: Bienvenido, Pablo. Te enseño el piso.
lines.english: Welcome, Pablo. Let me show you the flat.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.spanish: Esta es la cocina. Es pequeña pero tiene todo.
lines.english: This is the kitchen. It's small but has everything.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.spanish: El baño está al fondo del pasillo. Lo compartimos.
lines.english: The bathroom is at the end of the hall. We share it.

lines.speaker: Pablo
lines.english: Do you live alone or do you have family here?

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.spanish: Mis padres viven en Sevilla. Mi hermana vive cerca, a dos calles.
lines.english: My parents live in Seville. My sister lives nearby, two streets away.

lines.speaker: Pablo
lines.spanish: Mi familia está lejos, en Buenos Aires. Los echo de menos.
lines.english: My family is far away, in Buenos Aires. I miss them.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I have two brothers' in Spanish?
_____ padres viven en Colombia. (My parents live in Colombia)
Which is correct for 'Our house is big'?
How do you say 'The kitchen is small'?
quiz.explanation: 'Cocina' is feminine, so the adjective is 'pequeña' (not 'pequeño').

quiz.question: Arrange: hermana / mi / mayor / es / María
